title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the refrigerator. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Keeping Cool: The Cold Chain Revolution
content.text: Before the 20th century, the way we ate was dictated by the seasons and our proximity to the farm. Fresh meat and dairy were luxuries that had to be consumed almost immediately, while fruits and vegetables were preserved through drying, salting, or pickling. The ability to artificially control temperature would eventually change the global diet, but it began with a massive harvesting operation. ||(41)|| [41]These 'ice kings' would cut huge blocks from frozen lakes, insulate them with sawdust, and ship them as far as India and the Caribbean[/41]. While it was a booming industry, it was also highly inefficient; nearly half the ice would melt before reaching its destination. ||(42)|| [42]This quest for a more reliable method led to the development of mechanical refrigeration[/42]. In 1834, Jacob Perkins built the first working vapor-compression system, using a volatile liquid to absorb heat from its surroundings. However, these early machines were massive, dangerous, and used toxic chemicals like ammonia and sulfur dioxide. ||(43)|| [43]The true domestic revolution arrived in the 1920s with the invention of Freon[/43]. This non-toxic, non-flammable gas allowed manufacturers like General Electric to produce the first 'monitor top' refrigerators for the average home. Suddenly, the icebox was replaced by a sleek, electric appliance that didn't require a daily delivery from the ice man. ||(44)|| [44]The impact on the household was profound[/44]. It allowed families to shop for groceries once a week rather than every day, and it introduced the world to the convenience of 'leftovers.' This social shift was mirrored on an industrial scale by the creation of the 'cold chain.' ||(45)|| [45]This network of refrigerated trucks, ships, and warehouses meant that perishable goods could be transported across continents without spoiling[/45]. It allowed for the rise of the supermarket and the year-round availability of out-of-season produce, effectively erasing the geographical limits of the food supply. ||(46)|| [46]Today, we are facing the environmental consequences of this convenience[/46]. The old refrigerants contributed to the hole in the ozone layer, and modern cooling systems are major consumers of electricity. As we look to the future, the challenge is to maintain our cold chain while finding more sustainable ways to keep our food—and our planet—cool.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] the 'ice trade' of the 19th century [/41] was a global phenomenon. Companies in New England and Norway became incredibly wealthy by selling the winter’s harvest to the sweltering cities of the south.
paragraphs.B: This [42] dependency on natural ice [/42] was a constant source of frustration for brewers and meatpackers. A mild winter meant a 'famine' of ice, which could lead to thousands of tons of spoiled food and the collapse of entire businesses.
paragraphs.C: Because [43] of these safety concerns [/43], refrigeration remained a specialized industrial tool for decades. It was used in breweries and slaughterhouses, but it was considered far too risky to be installed in a private kitchen or a small grocery store.
paragraphs.D: This [44] accessibility of the technology [/44] led to a massive surge in demand. By the 1930s, the refrigerator had become a status symbol of the modern middle-class home, a testament to the idea that science could make domestic life easier and cleaner.
paragraphs.E: This [45] logistics breakthrough [/45] was the final link in the system. It connected the producer directly to the consumer, regardless of the distance, and made the concept of a 'local' diet a thing of the past for much of the developed world.
paragraphs.F: This [46] reliance on artificial cooling [/46] has also led to a rise in food waste. Because we can store so much for so long, we often forget about the items at the back of the shelf, leading to millions of tons of perfectly good food being thrown away every year.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] early food critics [/47] were skeptical of refrigerated food. They argued that meat kept on ice for weeks would lose its flavor and nutritional value, a fear that was eventually overcome by the sheer convenience and variety that the new system provided.

questions.explanation: A introduces the 'ice trade', which explains the 'massive harvesting operation' mentioned in the introduction.
B refers to the 'dependency on natural ice' and the problems it caused, setting up the need for the mechanical methods described next.
C refers to the 'safety concerns' (toxic chemicals) mentioned before the gap, explaining why the technology stayed in industries for so long.
D discusses the 'accessibility' of the refrigerator following the invention of Freon and the 'monitor top' model.
E explains the 'logistics breakthrough' (the cold chain), following the mention of industrial-scale shifts in the text.
F discusses the 'reliance on artificial cooling' and the resulting 'food waste', linking to the modern challenges mentioned in the final paragraph.
